originalText: "I'm five years too old to lie to myself and call it honor.
simplifiedText: She did not answer me.

originalText:
"

She didn’t answer.
simplifiedText: I felt angry, in love, and very sorry.

originalText: Angry, and half in love with her, and tremendously sorry, I turned away.
simplifiedText: Then, I looked away.

simplifiedText: One day in October, I saw Tom Buchanan.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: He was walking ahead of me along Fifth Avenue in his alert, aggressive manner, his hands slightly away from his body as if ready to fend off any disturbance, his head moving quickly in various directions, keeping pace with his restless gaze.
simplifiedText: Tom walked fast and looked around like he was ready for anything.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: Just as I slowed down to avoid catching up to him, he stopped and began frowning into the windows of a jewelry store.
simplifiedText: I slowed down so I wouldn't catch up to him.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: Suddenly, he saw me and walked back, extending his hand.
simplifiedText: He stopped and looked into a jewelry store window.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: "What's wrong, Nick?
simplifiedText: He saw me and came back, reaching out his hand.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: Do you have a problem with shaking hands with me?
simplifiedText: 'What's wrong, Nick? Don't you want to shake hands with me?'

originalText:

"Yes.

originalText:

He looked at me silently, confirming my suspicions about those lost hours.
simplifiedText: He looked at me without speaking, making me think my guess was right.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: As I began to walk away, he stepped closer and grabbed my arm.
simplifiedText: When I started to leave, he came close and held my arm.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: "I told him the truth," he declared.
simplifiedText: "I told him the truth," he said strongly.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: "He showed up at the door while we were getting ready to leave, and when I sent down a message that we weren't available, he tried to barge his way upstairs.
simplifiedText: "He came to the door as we were leaving, and when I said we were busy, he tried to force his way in.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: He was desperate enough to kill me if I hadn't told him who owned the car.
simplifiedText: He was so angry, he might have killed me if I didn't say who owned the car.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: He was gripping a revolver in his pocket the entire time he was in the house—" Tom stopped, his tone defiant.
simplifiedText: He had a gun in his pocket the whole time he was in the house," Tom said, sounding sure of himself.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: "So what if I did tell him?
simplifiedText: "So what if I told him? That man deserved it.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: That guy had it coming.
simplifiedText: He lied to you just like he lied to Daisy, but he was not a good man.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: He deceived you just as he did Daisy, but he was no saint.
simplifiedText: He hit Myrtle as if she was just a small, unimportant animal and didn't stop his car.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: He hit Myrtle like she was nothing more than a stray dog and didn’t even stop his car.
simplifiedText: Tom's words showed he didn't care about what happened.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText:
"

There was nothing I could say, except the one unutterable fact that it wasn't true.
simplifiedText: I could not say anything except it was not true.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: “And if you think I didn’t have my share of suffering—look here, when I went to give up that apartment and saw that damn box of dog biscuits sitting there on the sideboard, I sat down and cried like a baby.
simplifiedText: When I saw the dog biscuits, I cried a lot.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText:
By God, it was awful—”

I couldn’t forgive him or like him, but I saw that what he had done was, to him, entirely justified.
simplifiedText: I could not forgive him, but I understood why he did it.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: It was all very careless and confused.
simplifiedText: Tom and Daisy were careless and hurt others without caring.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText:
They were careless people, Tom and Daisy—they smashed up things and creatures and then retreated back into their wealth or their vast carelessness, or whatever it was that kept them together, and let other people clean up the mess they had made…

I shook hands with him; it felt absurd not to, as I suddenly found myself speaking to him as if he were a child.
simplifiedText: I shook hands with him, feeling like he was a child.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: Then he entered the jewelry store to purchase a pearl necklace—or maybe just a pair of cufflinks—freeing myself from my provincial reservations forever.
simplifiedText: He went to buy jewelry, and I felt free from my old fears.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText:
------------------------------------------------------------------------

Gatsby's house remained deserted when I left—its lawn had become as unkempt as my own.
simplifiedText: Gatsby's house was empty and not taken care of when I left.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: One of the cab drivers in the village never passed the entrance gate without pausing for a moment to point inside; perhaps he was the one who had driven Daisy and Gatsby to East Egg on the night of the tragedy, and maybe he had concocted his own version of the events.
simplifiedText: A cab driver always looked at Gatsby's house, maybe knowing its sad story.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: I didn't want to hear it and made sure to avoid him whenever I disembarked from the train.
simplifiedText: I avoided the cab driver because I did not want to hear his story.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: I spent my Saturday nights in New York because those gleaming, dazzling parties of his were with me so vividly that I could still hear the music and the laughter, faint and incessant, from his garden, and the cars going up and down his drive.
simplifiedText: I spent my Saturdays in New York, remembering Gatsby's parties.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: One night I did hear a car there, and saw its lights stop at his front steps.
simplifiedText: One night, I heard a car and saw its lights stop at Gatsby's front steps.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: But I didn’t investigate.
simplifiedText: But I didn't go to see who it was.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: Probably it was some final guest who had been away at the ends of the earth and didn’t know that the party was over.
simplifiedText: Maybe it was a guest who didn't know Gatsby's parties were over.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: On the last night, with my trunk packed and my car sold to the grocer, I went over and looked at that huge incoherent failure of a house once more.
simplifiedText: On my last night, with my things packed and my car sold, I looked at Gatsby's big, failed house again.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: On the white steps an obscene word, scrawled by some boy with a piece of brick, stood out clearly in the moonlight, and I erased it, drawing my shoe raspingly along the stone.
simplifiedText: Someone had written a bad word on the white steps with a brick, and I cleaned it off with my shoe.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: Then I wandered down to the beach and sprawled out on the sand.
simplifiedText: Then I walked to the beach and lay down on the sand.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: Most of the big shore places were closed now and there were hardly any lights except the shadowy, moving glow of a ferryboat across the Sound.
simplifiedText: Most big houses by the shore were closed and it was mostly dark except for a ferryboat's lights.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: And as the moon rose higher the inessential houses began to melt away until gradually I became aware of the old island here that flowered once for Dutch sailors’ eyes—a fresh, green breast of the new world.
simplifiedText: As the moon got higher, the houses seemed to disappear, and I saw the old island that was here before.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: Its vanished trees, the trees that had made way for Gatsby’s house, had once pandered in whispers to the last and greatest of all human dreams; for a transitory enchanted moment man must have held his breath in the presence of this continent, compelled into an aesthetic contemplation he neither understood nor desired, face to face for the last time in history with something commensurate to his capacity for wonder.
simplifiedText: The trees that were cut down for Gatsby's house once whispered of great dreams.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: And as I sat there reflecting on the ancient, undiscovered world, I thought of Gatsby's awe when he first noticed the green light at the end of Daisy's dock.
simplifiedText: Sitting there, I thought about how Gatsby felt when he first saw the green light at Daisy's dock.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: He had traveled a great distance to this blue lawn, and his dream must have seemed so near that he could almost seize it.
simplifiedText: Gatsby came far to reach his dream near this blue lawn.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: He didn't realize that it was already behind him, lost in that vast shadowy expanse beyond the city, where the dark fields of the republic stretched out under the night.
simplifiedText: He did not see his dream was already gone, lost far away past the city.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: Gatsby believed in the green light, the ecstatic future that year by year retreats before us.
simplifiedText: Gatsby hoped for a bright future, shown by the green light, that always moves away.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText: It escaped us then, but that's irrelevant—tomorrow we will run faster, stretch our arms wider...
simplifiedText: We thought we could catch it if we ran faster and reached further.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream

originalText:
And one fine morning—

So we continue, like boats against the current, carried back ceaselessly into the past.
simplifiedText: Like boats in a river, we keep moving back into the past.

chapter.title: The End of the Dream
chapter.theme: Gatsby's death and Nick's reflections on the American Dream
